text: Skeletal editing has emerged as a transformative frontier in synthetic organic chemistry, enabling the precise, atom-level modification of molecular cores. By facilitating the insertion, deletion, or exchange of individual atoms within a pre-existing scaffold, this "atom-level surgery" offers a streamlined alternative to resource-intensive de novo synthesis and provides a powerful tool for late-stage functionalization. This review highlights recent breakthroughs in the skeletal editing of carbo- and heterocyclic systems, with a particular emphasis on single-atom manipulations. We categorize the field into three primary strategic pillars: ring expansion via atom insertion, ring contraction through atom deletion, and transmutation via atom exchange. By integrating historical foundations with cutting-edge developments from the last five years—including carbene-mediated carbon insertions, nitrene-driven nitrogen scanning, and photochemical rearrangements—this evaluation explores the diverse mechanisms and insertive agents currently driving the field. Furthermore, we discuss the burgeoning area of stereochemical editing and isotopic exchange. By consolidating these advances, this review provides a holistic perspective on the current capabilities and future challenges of skeletal editing in the pursuit of molecular diversity for medicinal chemistry and materials science.
